coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots in baked lamb: Formation, cytotoxicity and scavenging capability to free radicals
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haitao
Xie, Yisha
Na, Xiaokang
Bi, Jingran
Liu, Shan
Zhang, Lijuan
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: The formation and properties of nanostructures during food processing have attracted much attention in recent years. Herein, the formation and physicochemical properties of fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) from baked lamb at different baking temperatures (200, 300, and 350 °C) were investigated. The morphology, surface functional groups and fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs were found to be highly dependent on the heating temperature. Biocompatibility of CDs investigation indicated that they were able to disperse onto both the cell membrane and the cytoplasm of HepG2 cells, and alter the cell cycle progression slightly. Moreover, the CDs from baked lamb of 350 °C showed the maximum scavenging capability to free radicals and could protect the cell from oxidative damage in vitro. This contribution represents the first report regarding the properties and formation process of CDs in baked lamb, providing valuable insights into baking temperature influence on physicochemical properties of the CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Baked lamb
Carbon dots
Cell oxidative damage
Free radicals

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of CDs from baked lamb
Free radical scavenging capability of CDs
Effects of baking temperature on CDs structure characteristics
Effects of baking temperature on physicochemical properties of CDs
Biocompatibility of CDs from baked lamb
CDs protecting HepG2 cells against H2O2-induced oxidative damage.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31601389 , 31872915 ), the Basic Research Program of Liaoning Education Department ( 2017J033 ), and the National Key Research and Development Project ( 2017YFC1600702 , 2017YFD0400103 , 2016YFD0400404 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (transmission electron microscope) image of CDs from baked lamb at 200 °C (A), 300 °C (C), and 350 °C (E), the inset image illustrates the lattice of the nanoparticle; size distribution histogram and Gauss fitting of particle size of CDs from baked lamb at 200 °C (B), 300 °C (D), and 350 °C (F).

ESR (electron spin resonance) spectra of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO)/OH adducts were collected after incubation 30 min of samples containing 0.6 mmol·L−1 EDTA-2Na-Fe2+ and 140 mmol·L−1 H2O2 in 150 mmol·L−1 pH = 7.4 phosphate buffer, and different concentrations of CDs from baked lamb at 200 °C (A), 300 °C (B), and 350 °C (C). OH scavenging activity of CDs from baked lamb (D). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Different letters marked on the columns mean there are significant differences (P < 0.05) among treatments of CDs from different temperature at the same concertation. The data were analyzed by using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). DMPO: 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide.

ESR (electron spin resonance) spectra of DPPH were obtained from samples containing 200 μmol·L−1 DPPH, 100 mmol·L−1 PB buffer (pH 6.0), and different concentrations of CDs from baked lamb at 200 °C (A), 300 °C (B), and 350 °C (C). DPPH radicals scavenging activity of CDs from baked lamb (D). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Different letters marked on the columns mean there are significant differences (P < 0.05) among treatments of CDs from different temperature at the same concentration. The data were analyzed by using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). DPPH: 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl.

CDs from baked lamb at 200 °C, 300 °C, and 350 °C influence on apoptosis HepG2 map. Q1 indicate the percentage of necrosis cell; Q2 indicate the percentage of late apoptosis cell; Q3 indicate the percentage of early apoptosis cell; Q4 indicate the percentage of the living cell;

The effect of CDs from baked lamb at 200 °C, 300 °C, and 350 °C on HepG2 cells cell cycle. G1 indicate the percentage of cell in G1 phase; G0 indicate the percentage of cell in G0 phase; S indicate the percentage of cell in S phase.

Damage effect of H2O2 on HepG2 cells (A). Oxidative stress was induced by exposing the cells to 700 μmol·L−1 H2O2 for 2 h. Meanwhile, the protective by CDs with 0–0.5 mg·mL−1 (B). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Asterisk was marked for which P < 0.05 compared to control groups in a two-tailed Student t test.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots in baked lamb: Formation, cytotoxicity and scavenging capability to free radicals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Seafood of Ministry of Education of China, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Haitao Wang and Yisha Xie contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The formation and properties of nanostructures during food processing have attracted much attention in recent years. Herein, the formation and physicochemical properties of fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) from baked lamb at different baking temperatures (200, 300, and 350 °C) were investigated. The morphology, surface functional groups and fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs were found to be highly dependent on the heating temperature. Biocompatibility of CDs investigation indicated that they were able to disperse onto both the cell membrane and the cytoplasm of HepG2 cells, and alter the cell cycle progression slightly. Moreover, the CDs from baked lamb of 350 °C showed the maximum scavenging capability to free radicals and could protect the cell from oxidative damage in vitro. This contribution represents the first report regarding the properties and formation process of CDs in baked lamb, providing valuable insights into baking temperature influence on physicochemical properties of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne nanoparticles have drawn massive attention from both academic and industrial communities for their unique characteristics that are distinct from their bulk materials. For example, TiO2 in nanosized scale has been added to foods such as powdered food, candies, and white sauces as brightening agent (Chen et al., 2013; Peters et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2014). Silica dioxide nanoparticles were used in the formation and stabilization of food emulsions (Dargahi-Zaboli, Sahraei, & Pourabbas, 2017; Dickinson, 2012). In addition to engineered nanoparticles that are added to food as additive, nanostructures with unique nano-effects could be produced during thermal food processing (Bi et al., 2018; Li, Bi, et al., 2017). However, almost all studies regarding nanoparticles in food have focused on artificially engineered nanomaterials that are added to food as additive (Chen, Seiber, & Hotze, 2014; Huang et al., 2017), which do not include the nanoparticles originating from food ingredients during processing. Therefore, it is very important to explore characteristics of these kinds of nanomaterial in food.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of carbon nanostructure which are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, nitrogen and/or other elements. They are spherical in shape with size below 10 nm, and could emit bright fluorescence under UV excitation (Cayuela, Soriano, Carrillo-Carrion, & Valcarcel, 2016; Song, Zhu, & Yang, 2014; Zhu et al., 2015). The presence of CDs in food was firstly reported in roasted bread by Sk, Jaiswal, Paul, Ghosh, and Chattopadhyay (2012) and the formation of these CDs was attributed to heating of the starting material. This phenomenon was further demonstrated by our and other research groups (Al-Hadi, Periasamy, Athinarayanan, & Alshatwi, 2016; Jiang et al., 2014). Thus, CDs originating from food ingredients during food processing is a distinct kind of nanostructures which are different from the engineered nanoparticles used as food additives. However, the properties of CDs in food is not yet fully understood.

Materials in nanosize are highly active due to the size and interface effects, which may cause biological toxicity. CDs are usually considered as safe materials since they are composed of no toxic elements. However, evidence has emerged that CDs may lead to adverse effect. For example, previous study of graphene quantum dots has shown that CDs induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, apoptosis, autophagy, and inflammatory response in THP-1 activated macrophages (Qin et al., 2015). Furthermore, recent results demonstrated that foodborne CDs induces metabolic stress in human cells (Al-Hadi et al., 2016). Generally, the toxic characters of nanomaterials are determined by their surface groups (Liu et al., 2010; Zhao et al., 2011), and the same is true of CDs (Havrdova et al., 2016). For CDs, the surface group properties were highly dependent on the reaction conditions and the starting materials (Wei et al., 2014). The artificial CDs were typically obtained through hydrothermal methods or concentrated acidic oxidation methods, which are quite different from food processing conditions. In parallel, formation process of CDs has been intensely investigated by using small organic molecules as carbon precursors. For instance, Zhu et al. characterized the CDs synthesized through hydrothermal method by using citric acid and ethylenediamine as carbon precursor, the results suggested that polymer-like CDs emerged first and ultimately converted to carbogenic CDs (Zhu et al., 2013). Our group has reported the formation of CDs in the Maillard reaction from glucose and lysine, and the CD surface groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups were found to possibly enable CDs to scavenge 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and hydroxyl radicals (Li, Na, et al., 2018). Food is a complex system that contains various macromolecules such as protein, lipid, and carbohydrate, which is distinct from small organic molecules, therefore, the reaction pathways for CDs generation may also be different from those small organic molecules. Thus, the systematic investigation of potential adverse effects of CDs from food is urgent and important. The results may help to improve the knowledge regarding nanomaterials in food.

We have demonstrated the presence of CDs in food items (Jiang et al., 2014; Jiang, Sun, Zhang, Wang, et al., 2015; Liao et al., 2015) and also investigated their properties. More recently, the presence and formation of CDs in protein-based food were studied and the results suggested that their properties were largely dependent on heating temperature and raw materials employed (Bi et al., 2018; Li, Bi, et al., 2017). However, the effects of temperature on the structures and characteristics are still unknown. The potential side effect is of great significance to bridge the existing gap between the properties of foodborne CDs and the corresponding processing conditions.

In this study, the effects of baking temperatures on structures and chemical properties of CDs from baked lambs were investigated for the first time. The physicochemical properties of CDs obtained at different temperatures were characterized. Furthermore, the effects of CDs on cell cycle and cell viability were conducted. Moreover, electron spin resonance (ESR) spin trapping methods were introduced to evaluate the free radical scavenging activities of CDs from lamb. Eventually, the in vitro antioxidant abilities of these CDs were further demonstrated by using HepG2 cells as a model.

Fresh lamb was selected from a supermarket in Dalian China. Hydrogen peroxide, ferrous sulfate, quinine sulfate, sodium chloride, and EDTA disodium salt were purchased from Aladdin technology (Shanghai, China). 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), DPPH and 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazoyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and minimum essential medium (MEM) were purchased from Hyclone (Logan, UT, USA). All other chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh lamb was baked in an electric oven at different temperatures (200, 300, or 350 °C) for 30 min. The CDs were extracted by dipping baked lamb in ethanol for 24 h with constant stirring. After removing the impurities by centrifugation, a vacuum rotary evaporator was used to remove the ethanol. The crude products were dissolved in water and the hydrophobic impurities were removed by extraction with chloroform (1:4, by volume). The CDs from baked lamb were further purified by dialyzing against deionized water with membrane of molecular weight cut-off 3500 Da for 2 days. The CDs were lyophilized and stored at 4 °C for further characterization. The morphology, optical properties, and chemical composition of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope, fluorescent spectrophotometer, fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer, and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) as described previously (Wang et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging capability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The scavenging of hydroxyl and DPPH radicals was measured by a Bruker A200 ESR spectrometer (Bruker BioSpin, Billerica, MA, USA) at ambient temperature.

For hydroxyl radical scavenging activity determination, the data were measured with 0.721 mW microwave power, 1 G field modulation amplitude. Using EDTA-2Na-Fe2+ (0.6 mmol·L−1) and H2O2 (140 mmol·L−1) to produce hydroxyl radicals within pH = 7.4 phosphate buffer (150 mmol·L−1, pH = 7.4), and, the OH was captured by DMPO (50 mmol·L−1). After mixing at 40 °C for 30 min, the mixtures were measured in quartz capillary tubes by ESR. The amount of OH was quantitatively estimated by the height of the second peak of the ESR spectrum (Zhang, Wang, Mo, & Qi, 2013).

For DPPH radical scavenging activity determination, the data were measured with 5.90 mW microwave power, 1 G field modulation amplitude. DPPH (20 μL, 200 μmol·L−1) in ethanol was mixed with 20 μL of phosphate buffer (100 mmol·L−1, pH 6.0) and 10 μL of CDs at different concentrations, after a 30 min incubation in the dark and then ESR spectra were collected. The amount of DPPH was quantitated by the height of the third peak.

HepG2 cells were cultured in MEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. For in vitro bio-distribution studies, the HepG2 cells were seeded in a tissue culture plate with the density of 1 × 106 cells and incubated for 24 h in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Then the CDs from baked lamb were introduced to the medium with a final concentration of 1 mg·mL−1 and incubated for 7 h. Finally, the cells were washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline. Cells imaging was performed on a SP8 laser scanning confocal microscopy (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany) with excitation wavelengths of 405, 458, and 488 nm.

Cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by cell cycle analysis and Annexin V-FITC/PI Staining. Briefly, HepG2 cells were seeded in 6-well plate at an initial cell density of 2 × 105 cells mL−1. Cells were harvested by trypsin after treated with CDs. For cell cycle analysis, cells were fixed in 70% ethanol and stained by propidium iodide. For cell apoptosis analysis, cells were washed twice with PBS and suspended in binding buffer, Annexin V-FITC and propidium iodide were added to stain apoptotic cells. The fluorescence was detected by a flow cytometer (BD, Franklin Lakes, USA).

Cell viability was evaluated using an MTT assay. The response of HepG2 cells to exposure to H2O2 was used to establish the models of oxidative damage. The HepG2 cells were seeded in the 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well, and the cell cultivation conditions as same as above. The cells were then treated with 700 μmol·L−1 H2O2. Different concentrations of CDs (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 mg·mL−1) were added to the medium at the same time and incubated for 2 h before cell viability assay. Then, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg·mL−1) were added and incubated for 3 h. DMSO (150 μL) was added after the removal of the supernatant. The absorbance at 570 nm was determined by an Infinite 200 multi-mode microplate reader (Tecan, Hombrechtikon, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of baking temperature on CDs structure characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The structures and optical properties of CDs are believed to be closely related to baking temperature. To justify this assumption, CDs from baked lamb roasted at 200, 300, and 350 °C were extracted and purified in order to explore the temperature influence on their physicochemical properties. The morphology of CDs characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) indicated that all the CDs from baked lamb at 200, 300, and 350 °C (Fig. 1 A–C) showed nearly spherical shape and dispersed uniformly without any apparent aggregation. The particle size of CDs decreased with the increment of baking temperatures. The average particle size of the CDs obtained at 200, 300 and 350 °C was approximately 2.78, 1.9, and 1.69 nm, respectively, based on the statistical analysis of 100 particles (Fig. 1D–F). The chemical composition of the lamb used in this study was shown in Table S1. A reasonable hypothesis is that the proteins, lipid, and carbohydrate in lamb were decomposed into nanostructures at low temperatures, and the nanostructures were carbonized subsequently with the elevation of temperature and small CDs were eventually formed. In addition, discernible lattice structure was observed in high resolution TEM images as shown by inset of Fig. 1A–C. The lattice spacing was 0.3, 0.18, and 0.18 nm, respectively.

The effects of baking temperature on surface chemical groups of CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. The typical absorption (Fig. S.1A) at around 3300 cm−1 was observed in all the CDs from baked lamb which was associated with the stretching vibration of the NH and OH. The peak at 2963 cm−1 corresponds to the CH vibrations, which became stronger with the increase of baking temperature, indicating the formation of methylene on the surface of CDs. In addition, bands near 1670 and 1599 cm−1 were identified as the CO stretching (Wang, Wang, & Chen, 2012) and amide NH bending, respectively. The relative intensity of band at 1670 cm−1 increased while the one at 1599 cm−1 decreased at higher baking temperatures, suggesting the partial formation of CO and breaking-down of NH with the rising of temperature. Additionally, the peak at 1445 cm−1 was attributed to CN stretching vibration, its strengthening indicating N atoms have doped into the framework of CDs during the baking process at high temperatures.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were introduced to further characterize the elemental composition and surface groups of CDs from baked lamb. The XPS survey spectra of CDs from different temperatures showed three predominant peaks at 285, 400, and 532 eV (Fig. S.1B–D), which were attributed to three elements, namely C, N, and O, respectively. These results suggest that the elemental composition of CDs obtained at different temperatures was similar. Furthermore, their relative contents were calculated based on the integral area and the results are shown in Table S2. The C content increased from 68.94 to 74.72% with a decrement of O content from 22.74 to 17.11% with elevating the baking temperature. Unlike C and O, the content of N in CDs was almost unaffected by baking temperature (ranging between 7.12 and 10.06%). These results revealed that the carbonization degree of CDs increased with the elevation of temperature.

The high resolution XPS spectra of the CDs from baked lamb at different temperatures are shown in Fig. S.2. The C1s XPS spectra confirmed the presence of CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds (Jiang, Sun, Zhang, Lu, et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2012) with the binding energy at 284.5, 285.5, 286.4, and 288.6 eV. The N1s high-resolution spectra exhibit characteristic peaks of amino N and pyrrolic-like N at 399.3, 400.0 eV, respectively (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016). The O1s XPS spectra showed peaks at ca. 531.4, 532.3 and 533.2 eV, indicating the presence of *OCO, CO, and OCO* (Chen, Jin, Wu, Tung, & Tang, 2014; Ding et al., 2016). Thus, the results from FT-IR and XPS provided solid evidence for the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs from baked lamb. Furthermore, the XPS intensity at 285.5 and 288.6 eV increased gradually from CDs obtained at 200 °C to those obtained at 350 °C, indicating a corresponding increase in the content of CO and CN in the CDs (Table S3), consistent with the results from FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of baking temperature on physicochemical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption, steady-state fluorescent spectra, lifetime, QY, and fluorescent stability were fully investigated in order to further explore the optical properties of CDs extracted from baked lamb at different temperatures. These CDs exhibited two characteristic absorption peaks centered at ca. 240 and 325 nm at a concentration of 0.015 mg·mL−1 (Fig. S.3A), which can be assigned to π–π* (aromatic CC) and n–π (CO and/or CN) transitions, respectively (Wang & Zhou, 2014; Xu et al., 2014). It is notable that the absorption around 325 nm increased as the baking temperature increased. This could be attributed to the formation of CO and/or CN, which further demonstrated the results from FT-IR and XPS.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs obtained from the lamb baked at 200, 300, and 350 °C (Fig. S.3B–D) showed that all CDs exhibited an obvious excitation-dependent fluorescence, and the maximum emission peak red-shifted along with the increase of excitation wavelength. The classical bathochromic emission phenomenon might be attributed to different emitting centers present in CDs. The maximum fluorescent emission wavelength of CDs from the lamb baked at 200, 300, and 350 °C was 401, 388, and 385 nm, respectively. This result suggested that the rising of baking temperature could lead to the blue-shifts of the maximum emission.

The lifetime was measured at room temperature by dispersing CDs in aqueous solution. Fluorescence decay profile of CDs obtained at 200, 300, and 350 °C can be fitted using a two-exponential function with an average lifetime of 8.2, 9.99, 9.1 ns, respectively (Fig. S.4), which is comparable to previously reported CDs (Ding et al., 2016). Baking temperature showed no obvious influence on lifetimes of CDs. In addition, the long lifetime component was dominated in all CDs obtained at different temperatures, which was attributed to the surface states (Long et al., 2012). The fluorescence QY of the CDs produced at 200, 300, and 350 °C was determined to be 6, 15, 45% (Fig. S.5) using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol·L−1 H2SO4 as a reference. Generally, the presence of functional groups on the CDs surface is a key factor that determined their QY (Zhu et al., 2015). The temperature-dependence of QY may be attributed to the formation of trapping excitons on the surface of the CDs as the baking temperature increased.

The effects of pH, ionic strength, and continuous illumination on fluorescence intensity of lamb borne CDs were investigated systemically in order to evaluate their stability. All the CDs obtained from baked lamb showed a relatively stable emission at broad ranges of pH (4–9), and significant decrease of fluorescent intensity was observed only under extreme pH conditions (pH higher than 10 or lower than 4, Fig. S.6A). This result suggested protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups on CDs that obtained at different temperatures were similar under various pH conditions. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of the lamb borne CDs was constant up to an ionic strength of 250 mmol·mL−1 NaCl (Fig. S.6B), indicating their excellent salt tolerance nature. Besides, the fluorescence emission of CDs obtained from lambs was stable under continuous UV radiation (under their maximum excitation wavelength), and a slight decrease of fluorescent intensity occurred (about 1%) after an 1800 s irradiation (Fig. S.6C). It is noteworthy that the decreasing degree of fluorescent intensity from baked lamb was negatively correlated with heating temperature. Since the surface groups are easily etched by UV light, surface-state fluorescence is more sensitive to photobleaching than center-state fluorescence (Song, Zhu, Xiang, et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2015). The temperature dependence photobleaching phenomenon provided further evidence that carbonization degree of CDs from lamb increased with the elevated baking temperature.

Considering the properties of CDs from baked lamb were highly affected by baking temperature, as described above, it is reasonable to conjecture that their free radical scavenging activity was also dependent on processing conditions. Therefore, to analyze the free radical scavenging activity deeply, a set of cell-free radical producing systems was developed for measuring the common free radicals using ESR.

Hydroxyl radical (OH) is highly reactive and can lead to oxidative damage of biomacromolecule (Chong et al., 2016). Hydroxyl radicals are generated by Fenton reaction (Chong et al., 2016) and their concentration is determined by using DMPO as a trapping agent. The formation of DMPO/OH adduct gives strong ESR signals and its intensity decreased significantly upon the addition of lamb borne CDs baked at 200, 300 and 350 °C (Fig. 2 ). Furthermore, the hydroxyl radical scavenging capacity of CDs from lamb was compared and the result is shown in Fig. 2D. Generally, hydroxyl radical scavenging capacity of CDs from lamb is temperature-dependent, and the highest hydroxyl radicals scavenging capacity was achieved when heating at 350 °C. Hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of CDs from 350 °C baked lamb was approximately 43% at a concentration of 8 mg·mL−1 using 1 mg mL−1 ascorbic acid as the positive control in hydroxyl radical scavenging activity assessment (Fig. S.7A and B).

DPPH radical is a type of nitrogen-centered free radical that is distinct from ROS. The DPPH radical scavenging activity is a basic indicator for evaluating the anti-radical activity of a large variety of compounds (Sharma & Bhat, 2009). The intensity of ESR signal from DPPH radicals decreased significantly when CDs obtained from baked lamb were introduced (Fig. 3 A–C). Similar to hydroxyl radical scavenging activity, CDs obtained at higher baking temperatures tended to display greater scavenging capacity against DPPH radicals. The DPPH radicals were almost totally quenched when the concentration of CDs exceed 4 mg mL−1, irrespective of the baking temperature (Fig. 3D), using 1 mg mL−1 ascorbic acid as the positive control in DPPH radical scavenging activity assessment (Fig. S.7C and D).

The results described above confirm the free radical scavenging activity of CDs from baked lamb. Previous studies regarding free radical quenching activities of CDs were mainly focused on investigating fluorescence mechanisms of CDs and their potential application in sensors. For example, Song et al. (2014) investigated the fluorescence center of a typical CDs that was synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine by using hydroxyl radical as the fluorescence quenching agent. Wang, Lu, Hou, Liu, and Zhang (2016) found that CDs could apparently be quenched by hydroxyl radicals and designed a highly sensitive detection strategy for uric acid in human serum samples by combining uricase and CDs. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no studies about free radical scavenging activity of CDs from foods, much less to investigate the effects of processing conditions on their free radical scavenging activity. In this study, lamb was taken as an example to investigate the effects of processing conditions on free radical scavenging capacities of CDs from food. The results revealed that higher baking temperatures tended to increase free radical scavenging activity of CDs, irrespective of the CDs obtained at 200 °C. The higher hydroxyl radical scavenging capacity of CDs obtained at high temperatures may be related to their smaller size (Zhang, Wang, Koberstein, Khalid, & Chan, 2004). As described above, high baking temperature leads to the formation of CDs with small size. A smaller size can offer a higher specific surface area under a certain concentration and facilitates interactions between CDs and free radicals. Therefore, CDs obtained at high baking temperatures scavenging free radicals were more effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: Previous reports have demonstrated that CDs can be uptake by cells and interfere with cellular metabolism (Li, Li, et al., 2018). The effects of CDs from baked lambs on cell were unknown. Since human could have exposed to those CDs by eating, the evaluation of cytotoxicity of CDs from baked lambs is extremely important. Cellular distributions of CDs were investigated firstly by using HepG2 cell as an example. After being induced by CDs for 7 h, confocal microscope image showed that the emission mainly located within the HepG2 cells (Fig. S.8). What’s more, CDs located in HepG2 cells emit multi-colorful fluorescence when excited at 405, 458, and 488 nm, respectively, due to the unique bathochromic emission phenomenon. Next, the cytotoxicity of CDs was investigated by employing Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining. Fig. 4 shows that the tested CDs exhibited extremely low cytotoxicity, i.e., viability more than 90% at a concentration of 2 mg·mL−1 after 4 h incubation. It was important to note that the early apoptotic rate of HepG2 was dependent on the formation temperature of CDs. The highest apoptosis rate was 6.94% when treated with CDs from the highest baking temperature, while the early apoptotic rates were lower after incubated with CDs from baked lamb at 300 and 200 °C, which were 4.81% and 6.16%, respectively. This result was similar to CDs from grilled hamburger, which shown greater cytotoxicity when processed at a higher temperature. This result might be caused by the different carbonization degree of the CDs at different temperatures (Li, Bi, et al., 2017). Mammalian cell cycle is a highly organized and tightly regulated process that ensures accurate gene replication and cell division. Cell cycle arrest could be observed before an obvious decrease of cell viability after incubating with nanomaterials (Gao et al., 2016). Cell cycle analysis was performed in order to further evaluate the potential adverse effects of CDs from baked lamb. HepG2 cells were influenced by CDs obtained from baked lambs. As shown in Fig. 5 , G0/G1 phase decreased by approximately 8.66%, 11.03%, and 10.87%, respectively, after treated with CDs from baked lamb at 200, 300, and 350 °C, compared with the control group. S phase cells increased by approximately 14.85%, 10.05%, and 6.5%, respectively at the same condition. This indicates that CDs from baked lambs lead to S phase arrest which may inhibit cell proliferation (Qiu et al., 2014). The above experiments indicated that the CDs from baked lamb have excellent biocompatibility under experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs protecting HepG2 cells against H2O2-induced oxidative damage.
ce:section.ce:para: Free radicals could generate during cellular metabolism. When the production of free radicals exceeds the antioxidant capacity of cellular antioxidant system, oxidative stress occurs. Many researchers have noticed that CDs exhibit free radical quenching activities. For example, Zhao et al. demonstrated that CDs synthesized from garlic by hydrothermal processing exhibited radical scavenging activity (Wang et al., 2015). Chong et al. studied the antioxidant characteristics of CDs and their results suggested that these CDs could scavenge a number of free radicals efficiently and protect cells against oxidative damage (Chong et al., 2016). More recently, Selenium-doped CDs were demonstrated as an effective free radical scavenging agent as well and successfully rescue cell from oxidative stress (Li, Li, et al., 2017). Since the excellent free radical scavenging capacity and the excellent biocompatibility, the CDs from baked lambs were speculated to be taken up by cells and subsequently protect them against oxidative damage. In order to confirm the assumption, a damage model was introduced to evaluate the protective effect of CDs on oxidative stress. The HepG2 cells growth was inhibited with the increase of H2O2 (Fig. 6 A). The cell viability achieved was approximately 50% of the control group at the H2O2 concentration of 700 μmol·L−1 after a 2 h incubation period. Hence, the H2O2-induced oxidative stress model with HepG2 cells was successfully constructed for subsequent evaluation of protection effect. Fig. 6B shows the protective effects of CDs on H2O2-induced oxidative damage HepG2 cells. The cell viability of CDs treated groups was improved significantly when compared with the H2O2 treated group without CDs. It indicates that the CDs have certain protective effects on H2O2-induced oxidative damage HepG2 cells and showed oxidative stress inhibitory activity.

In summary, this work demonstrated the formation, cytotoxicity and scavenging capability to free radicals of CDs in baked lamb. The morphology, surface functional groups, free radical scavenging capacities and fluorescent properties of CDs from baked lamb were highly dependent on baking temperature. Biocompatibility of CDs was investigated using flow cytometry, and the results revealed that the toxicity of CDs was weak. In addition, the in vitro protection of CDs on cell oxidative damage was demonstrated using HepG2 cells as a model. These findings of the effects of baking temperature on properties of CDs provide new knowledge about the formation of fluorescent nanostructures in baked food items as well as physicochemical properties.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31601389, 31872915), the Basic Research Program of Liaoning Education Department (2017J033), and the National Key Research and Development Project (2017YFC1600702, 2017YFD0400103, 2016YFD0400404).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.02.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
